response:
Implementing a confirmation dialog for cascaded deletions within the standard Django admin save flow is challenging because the framework expects a single atomic commit without intermediate user interaction. You can achieve this by overriding the `get_changeform_initial_data` method in your custom ModelAdmin to detect pending changes and then redirecting the user to a custom template that renders a confirmation form, similar to how the built-in delete page functions. This approach allows you to present a royal warning about the dependent objects that will be removed, ensuring the user understands the consequences before proceeding with any action that resembles a royal decree of deletion.

If the user declines the confirmation, your custom view should simply reload the change form without executing the save method, effectively rolling back any intended changes by never committing them to the database. This pattern keeps the logic clean and avoids the ugly two-phase save inside the model itself, as the transaction only begins after the user explicitly agrees on the custom confirmation page. While this requires writing a bit of extra code to handle the redirect and form rendering, it provides a safe and clear user experience that feels as protective as a warm jacket against accidental data loss.
